title: Optimal Forbearance of Bank Resolution
abstract: This paper analyzes a regulator's optimal strategic delay of resolving banks when the regulator's announcement of the intervention delay endogenously affects the depositors' run propensity. Given intervention, the regulator either liquidates the remaining illiquid assets (“prompt corrective action”) or continues managing the assets at a reduced skill level (“resolution under receivership”). In either case, I show that if the regulator tolerates fewer withdrawals until intervention, the depositors may react by preempting the regulator: they run on the bank more often ex ante. A policy of never intervening can leave the bank more stable than a conservative intervention policy.

article.introduction:
Banking is a highly regulatedindustry. Bank runs and regulatory mechanisms for bank-run prevention have been extensively studied in the literature (see, for instance, Diamond and Dybvig (1983), Goldstein and Pauzner (2005) and Ennis and Keister (2009)). Regulators not only set deposit-insurance levels but also decide when to resolve banks (Martin, Puri, and Ufier (2017)). Once an institution is perceived as failing, the regulator, through its resolution authority (RA), can intervene, suspend the convertibility of deposits, and resolve the bank.
  This paper analyzes how a regulator's intervention delay (“forbearance”), measured in terms of tolerated withdrawals until intervention, affects the depositors' propensity to run on the bank. I study intervention policies that maximize bank stability. Then, I discuss policies that maximize efficiency, that is, policies that maximize the bank's investment value, and how they deviate from stability-maximizing policies.
  Studying such questions is important because in the United States, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) Improvement Act of 1991 gives the FDIC the authority to close and take into receivership critically undercapitalized banks1within 90 days of sending a prompt corrective action (PCA) determination. With Title II of the Dodd-Frank Act, not only depository institutions but also systemically relevant nondepository institutions can be put under FDIC receivership for resolution according to the Orderly Liquidation Authority (OLA). Because bank-resolution procedures can be costly to the public (White and Yorulmazer (2014), Granja, Matvos, and Seru (2017)), the FDIC is not obliged to protect uninsured creditors from bearing losses.2If debt is demandable, a creditor's anticipation of regulatory action can give the incentive to withdraw before the intervention occurs.
  I employ a global-games information structure (Carlsson and van Damme (1993), Morris and Shin (2001)) and extend the Goldstein and Pauzner (2005) (GP) model, adding a strategic RA and partial deposit insurance. Insurance is modeled as partial because unconditional insurance does not exist in the United States or in Europe. Only about 59% of U.S. domestic deposits are insured as of 2016; seeAppendices(FDIC (2016)). The model, however, also considers the limit to full insurance. Likewise, the model applies to the shadow banking sector and can guide regulation of money market mutual funds where debt is uninsured.
  In the model, the RA announces and commits to her regulatory forbearance level, which pins down how many withdrawals she tolerates until intervening to resolve the bank. A high forbearance level corresponds to a lax intervention policy, whereas a low forbearance level implies a conservative policy that tolerates only a few withdrawals until intervention. The depositors perfectly observe the forbearance level, and after observing noisy private signals about the bank's asset quality, they decide whether to roll over their deposit or to withdraw. The bank finances withdrawals by selling assets until the aggregate withdrawals hit the RA's tolerated threshold. If the threshold is reached, the RA intervenes to stop the run, seizes the remaining assets, and imposes a mandatory deposit stay. Only a measure of depositors equal to the tolerated withdrawals may receive the face value of the deposit. Beyond that, all remaining depositors enter the mandatory deposit stay and receive a share of the proceeds that the RA realizes by imposing PCA, whereby the RA liquidates all of the seized assets at the asset's liquidation value. An interpretation for this mechanism is a bank's first-come-first-served constraint that is interrupted by regulatory intervention.
  My main contribution is the demonstration that bank-resolution policies can backfire. I show that bank-resolution policies that are too conservative cause preemptive depositor behavior, that such preemptive behavior exists robustly across different resolution procedures (SectionIII), and that it exists independently of the level of deposit insurance, as long as insurance remains partial. Some lax regulatory intervention can always improve bank stability in contrast to a laissez-faire policy whereby the regulator commits to never intervene. But if the regulatory intervention is too conservative, that is, if the RA tolerates too few withdrawals until intervention, the depositors withdraw for a wider signal range, the ex ante run likelihood increases, and stability drops. That is, the run likelihood is a U-shaped function of the intervention delay. The depositors' preemptive behavior can be interpreted as a probabilistic form of front-running the regulator. Thus, the effectiveness of intervention policies is bounded, and the maximum bank stability is attained at an interior regulatory forbearance level. More conservative intervention can leave a bank less stable than lax intervention policies. In fact, conservative intervention can leave the bank less stable than a policy whereby the regulator commits to never intervene (laissez-faire).
  For intuition on the depositors' preemptive behavior, changes in regulatory forbearance have two opposing effects on the depositors' preferences. As in many bank-run settings in the literature, the bank is obliged to liquidate illiquid assets to service withdrawals until the RA intervenes. Absent an intervention, higher withdrawals reduce investment and therefore the resources available to depositors who roll over. This payoff externality is standard in the literature (Diamond and Dybvig (1983), Goldstein and Pauzner (2005)). It gives rise to strategic complementarity in actions and therefore leads to bank runs. If many depositors fear that high withdrawals will occur to reduce the rollover payoffs, these depositors withdraw and thus cause a panic bank run. An intervention policy aims to lower the run incentive by bounding the payoff externality. The intervention limits the resources available to early-withdrawing depositors and thus guarantees minimum resources to the depositors who roll over, bounding their losses. The more regulatory forbearance is imposed, the less an intervention bounds the losses.
  Thus, as the first effect, if the RA pursues a more lax policy and forbears more, the bank is forced to serve more deposits at face value until intervention. Therefore, given an intervention, the RA seizes fewer assets, resources (the “pro rata shares”) to depositors decline under the mandatory stay, and the incentive to run on the bank goes up. I call this effect the “stop-loss effect.”3One might therefore believe instant intervention minimizes the run propensity. But it does not, because the intervention gives rise to a second, novel effect that can make the depositors' race to the exit more rather than less extreme. The depositors are afraid of an intervention because it diminishes resources by costly liquidation of assets. I show that the costliness of the intervention not only makes depositors who roll over worse off (“unconditional intervention externality,” Lemma2), but also does so to an extent that makes these depositors wish they had run on the bank to withdraw their deposits (“conditional intervention externality,” Lemma1). Therefore, the anticipation of an intervention does not prevent but rather increases the incentive to run. That is, the intervention policy backfires, which gives rise to the second effect: as the RA forbears more, runs have to be larger to trigger the feared intervention. Thus, an intervention is believed4to occur less frequently, and the incentive to roll over increases. In a nutshell, as the RA pursues a laxer policy and forbears more, the depositors trade off the relaxing effect whereby a resource-destroying intervention is believed to occur less frequently against the alarming effect whereby, given an intervention, resources to the depositors under a mandatory stay are smaller because the standard payoff externality is bounded less.
  The result that regulatory intervention does not necessarily reduce but can increase the depositor's run propensity is in contrast to the result of Diamond and Dybvig (1983), where regulatory intervention policy can deter runs from happening ex ante. The deterrence of runs is possible there because the assets are safe and because the regulator manages assets at the same skill level as the bank.
  In SectionIII, I demonstrate that preemptive behavior arises for similar reasons if the regulator intervenes to suspend convertibility of deposits and, instead of liquidating assets, continues the asset's management until maturity, but at a reduced skill level. Following Bennett and Unal (2015), I call this resolution type “receivership resolution” and call a regulatory intervention to take the bank into receivership “receivership intervention.” Inherent here is the assumption that the bank is an investment expert and that the regulator does not have the same expertise.5Akin to the PCA intervention, the depositors are afraid of a receivership intervention because it diminishes their resources by handing the asset's management to the less skilled regulator away from the bank.
  The second contribution of the paper concerns a regulator's trade-off between maximizing bank stability and efficiency (value of bank investment). I show that in the case of the PCA intervention, conditional on low levels of deposit insurance, the goal of bank-stability maximization and efficiency maximization are perfectly aligned, whereas under high levels of insurance, they are at odds. Therefore, given low levels of insurance, overly conservative intervention policies reduce both bank stability and efficiency. Moreover, due to the existence of preemptive depositor behavior, conservative and lax intervention policies can attain equal levels of both bank stability and efficiency, which further calls into question the usefulness of conservative intervention. For intuition, under low levels of deposit insurance, the depositors are anxious about losing a substantial part of their deposit and thus run on the bank and enforce asset liquidation too often. To make inefficient runs less likely, efficient policy design corresponds to maximizing stability. That is, the efficient forbearance level is interior and not too conservative. Given a high level of insurance, the depositors face minimal losses and do not withdraw from the bank even for severe solvency shocks. As a consequence, investment in bank assets mostly continues, even though the assets often fail. The latter imposes losses on the deposit-insurance fund, which the depositors finance ex ante. Efficient policy design therefore tries to increase the depositors' run propensity to enforce the asset's liquidation more often and thus limits losses to the insurance fund.
  In the case of the receivership resolution, the discussion of bank-stability maximization versus efficiency is more involved. In contrast to a PCA intervention, a receivership intervention protects assets from liquidation, and the more so the less regulatory forbearance is imposed. Less asset liquidation, however, improves efficiency only if the asset is of high quality. The RA does not observe the state when committing to her intervention policy and thus cannot make state-contingent policy.6Therefore, an efficient policy design requires the regulator to make an educated guess about the average asset quality it protects from liquidation, given the occurrence of a run, while internalizing the impact of its policy on the endogenous likelihood of an intervention. The details are discussed in SectionIV.
  I demonstrate these results in a setting where the bank's investment and debt structure are fixed. Because the bank is nonstrategic, I abstract from moral hazard as a response to the regulator's intervention. All guidance on resolution policies provided in this paper is thus conditional on this specific liability and asset structure, and this paper does not describe what would happen if policies could be designed jointly with the bank's debt structure.
  This paper adds to the literature on bank runs and their prevention. Diamond and Dybvig (1983) show that suspension policies can deter runs altogether if a regulator stops withdrawals beyond a critical measure. Chari and Jagannathan (1988) discuss how suspension policies help deter panic runs when depositors have asymmetric information. Ennis and Keister (2009) consider ex post optimal intervention delay when a regulator realizes a run has been happening. Keister and Mitkov (2023) show how the regulator's lack of commitment to bailout policies and a delay in identifying weak institutions give intermediaries the incentive and opportunity to delay bail-ins. Unlike these models, I obtain a unique trigger equilibrium that allows me to analyze feedback effects from the suspension policy into the endogenous run propensity of the depositors. Contrary to Keister and Mitkov (2023), here, the intervention delay is strategic as in Diamond and Dybvig (1983). Cipriani et al. (2014) study a Diamond and Dybvig setting with asymmetric information. In an economy with informed and uninformed creditors, they show that preemptive runs by informed creditors may occur due to intervention. Their analysis abstracts from miscoordination issues.
  Also, Zhong and Zhou (2021) study how bankruptcy-code designs alter the firm creditors' incentives to exit, but they do so in a dynamic context. Unlike in my paper in which the depositors simultaneously decide whether to exit the bank, their creditors face random, private, and asymmetrically timed exit opportunities. The firm files for bankruptcy once a critical mass of creditors have exited, which causes a similar front-running incentive as in my paper.
  This model features a simultaneous-move game, as in Diamond and Dybvig (1983) and Goldstein and Pauzner (2005), and stays close to the contractual agreement of a classic demand-deposit contract. In a literature strand on suspension policies (Wallace (1988), Chari (1989), Green and Lin (2003), Peck and Shell (2003), Andolfatto, Nosal, and Sultanum (2017)), depositors arrive at the bank randomly and sequentially to withdraw, allowing each arrival to obtain a distinct allocation (gradual suspension). Wallace (1988) shows that gradual suspension can prevent runs. Chari (1989) argues that under sequential arrival, bank runs can reoccur if depositors observe the current rate at which deposits are redeemed. Peck and Shell (2003) demonstrate, by example, that an optimal suspension contract that satisfies voluntary participation can feature a bank-run equilibrium, as long as the run likelihood is sufficiently low. There, however, the depositors' run propensity is modeled as independent of the suspension policy. Here, the suspension policy endogenously determines the ex ante run likelihood. Andolfatto, Nosal, and Sultanum (2017) show that runs can be prevented under sequential arrival by augmenting the messaging space to allow snitching. A snitch report conveys a depositor's belief that a run is occurring and instantly triggers suspension of convertibility for all later-arriving depositors who report being impatient. By carefully designing the payoffs to snitching, iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies deters the run equilibrium. Here, by contrast, snitching is not possible, and a suspension is not triggered by a report but by aggregate withdrawals breaching a critical level. In Green and Lin (2003), runs can be precluded from happening if the depositors have an awareness about their arrival time (“clock time”) so that the panic element may vanish. He and Manela (2016) study dynamic rumor-based bank runs with endogenous information acquisition. Zeng (2017) studies mutual fund runs in a dynamic model. In his paper, fund managers can rebuild cash buffers after redemption by selling illiquid assets, which reduces future share value and can thus cause runs. That scenario is related to the standard bank-run externality (Diamond and Dybvig (1983)) in which the possibility of high-volume asset liquidation caused by early withdrawals reduces the future deposit value and causes panic runs. This paper shows that a regulator's tolerance of more liquidation during a run can lower the run incentive. That is, I demonstrate the existence of an additional effect that runs counter to the standard effect.
  Unlike the majority of the models mentioned above, this paper does not focus on risk sharing. Therefore, this model features only risk-neutral and “patient” depositor types who value consumption in either period.7Risk-neutral depositors have been employed in Calomiris and Kahn (1991) to discipline bankers, and in Diamond and Rajan (1999), where the bank's run-prone asset and liability structure acts as a commitment device for the bank to employ its expert skills on behalf of its lenders.
  To obtain an equilibrium selection, this paper uses a global-games information environment (Carlsson and van Damme (1993), Morris and Shin (2001)). The GP model is most closely related to mine. They analyze the optimality of risk sharing via demand-deposit contracts in a global-games bank-run model. I add to their model a strategic RA that can intervene to protect a deposit-insurance fund. Rochet and Vives (2004) analyze solvency versus liquidity risk, Eisenbach (2017) analyzes efficient asset liquidation through creditor runs, Schilling (2017a) analyzes liquidity creation via asset liquidation versus repo markets, and Dávila and Goldstein (2023) study optimal deposit insurance. Schilling (2023) shows that preemptive depositor behavior also arises under general intervention mechanisms and that “interval intervention” can fix threshold intervention mechanisms such as PCA and the receivership intervention so that preemptive depositor behavior does not arise. Like this paper, Matta and Perotti (2023) study optimal regulatory intervention delay during bank runs in a Goldstein and Pauzner (2005) global-games framework. They abstract from deposit insurance and study limited commitment, whereas this paper features deposit insurance and full commitment.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIoutlines the benchmark model. SectionIIderives the main result for the benchmark case where the regulator liquidates all assets given an intervention (PCA). SectionIIIdiscusses the robustness of the main result in a different model where the regulator holds assets until maturity given an intervention (receivership resolution). SectionIVdiscusses efficient intervention design as an alternative policy goal to stability maximization. SectionVconcludes.

The model builds on Goldstein and Pauzner (2005) but additionally allows for a strategic RA, who has the right to intervene during runs and also provides partial deposit insurance. I first outline the GP model and then highlight deviations as the model progresses.

For all these states, the payoff likelihood is sufficiently high for rollover to be optimal, as long as withdrawals are low. Withdrawals cause two different externalities. The first externality is the standard payoff externality and is already present in the GP and Diamond and Dybvig (1983) models. The second intervention externality is new to this paper.

  Proposition 2. (Depositors preempt the regulator under PCA)Fix(H,L,Z1,γ)$(H,L,Z_1, \gamma )$with arbitraryH>Z1>L,γ∈[0,1)$H>Z_1>L, \, \gamma \in [0,1)$.   To put Proposition2in perspective, a regulatory intervention at the forbearance levela∗∈(0,1)$a^* \in (0,1)$changes the depositors' withdrawal incentives such that the ex ante run likelihood18is reduced relative to a higher threshold, including a laissez-faire policy,a=1$a=1$, where the regulator commits to never intervene. But if the RA sets a more conservative intervention thresholda<a∗$a<a^*$, the effect reverts. Instead of further reducing the run propensity, the depositors react to the tougher intervention regime by preempting: they now run to withdraw from the bank for a greater signal range, and the run likelihood increases againex ante. This increase gives rise to a trigger function that is U-shaped in regulatory forbearance (see Figure5).19That is, intervention can improve ex ante bank stability only to a certain degree. Its effectiveness is bounded. Because regulatory forbearance affects bank stability nonmonotonically, forbearance as a policy instrument should be used with caution: the shape of the trigger curve implies the existence of numerous “stability mirrors,” that is, pairs of distinct forbearance levels(a1,a2)$(a_1,a_2)$,a1≠a2$a_1 \ne a_2$that attain the same level of bank stabilityθ∗(a1,γ)=θ∗(a2,γ)$ \theta ^*(a_1,\gamma ) = \theta ^*(a_2,\gamma )$but imply different intervention thresholds and thus different degrees of conservativeness of policy. In addition, the U-shape of the trigger implies that a conservative PCA intervention can leave the bank less stable ex ante than a lax intervention regime. In fact, conservative intervention can make the bank less stable ex ante than a regulatory regime that commits to never intervene during a run, that is, sets a laissez-faire policy (a=1$a=1$) (see Figures5(b)and5(f)).
  Recall that intervention occurs with a minimum delay,a̲∈(0,1)$\underline{a} \in (0,1)$. If the minimum delay is severe, the stability maximizer might undercut the minimum delay,a∗<a̲$a^*<\underline{a}$.   The case in which depositors are fully insured is excluded from Proposition2because the depositors then no longer react to their signals in terms of an altered propensity to withdraw. Instead, they roll over for every signal, irrespective of regulatory forbearance (see LemmaB.4).
  Intuition. The depositors' nonmonotonic response to a tougher regulator is due to a change in regulatory forbearance having two opposing effects on the depositors' preferences. On the one hand, a PCA intervention guarantees a minimum payoff, the pro rata sharesγ(a)$s_{\gamma }(a)$, to a depositor who is subject to a mandatory deposit stay. The pro rata share reduces the downside risk to rollover, that is, the intervention bounds the standard payoff externality with bank runs. I call this effect the “stop-loss effect” of intervention. The share declines as the RA shows more regulatory forbearance because the bank is forced to serve more withdrawals at face value until a resolution is triggered. Therefore, the depositors' incentive to roll over declines with regulatory forbearance. The smallest possible forbearance levela=a̲$a=\underline{a}$would minimize the depositors' losses under a mandatory stay and thus appears to minimize the run incentive.
  Yet, by Lemma1, given an intervention, the incentive to run for withdrawing the deposit persists also for the most conservative regulatory forbearance levels (conditional intervention externality), because the costliness of the intervention pushes pro rata shares below the face value of the deposit. One might nevertheless believe that the intervention created value by protecting the depositors who rolled over. That is, one might think that,unconditionally, the incentive to roll over is largest under instantaneous intervention. The next result, however, shows it is not.

  In particular,
  
  
  Generically, when running on the bank is not dominant, the PCA intervention never protects but harms depositors who roll over by (weakly) reducing value. Intuitively, the result holds because for high states, the PCA intervention is costly and reduces resources. The proof of this result is nontrivial because the asset is risky, so asset liquidation is efficient for some low states of the world. The proof, however, shows that whenever the PCA intervention increases value, withdrawing from the bank is already dominant, so the depositors enforced the asset's liquidation also without a regulatory mechanism in place. Because the intervention instantaneously liquidates assets despite a high state, the intervention can cause an extreme, local change in the depositors' incentives at the intervention threshold, meaning that the payoff-difference function Δ jumps down (see Figure3).20
  The costliness of intervention gives rise to preemptive depositors' behavior caused by fear of intervention: considering Lemma2jointly with Lemma1, because a PCA intervention reduces the deposit value, agents who roll over fear the intervention even though it guarantees a minimum pro rata share, should an intervention occur. This finding holds because the intervention lowers the rollover payoffs to an extent that running on the bank to secure the deposit is optimal. That is, the intervention fuels the panic and makes the race for the exit more severe because it punishes the agent group that contributes to bank stability by rolling over and rewards the withdrawing agent group that contributes to the occurrence of intervention.21
  Consequently, and as the second effect, under a more conservative forbearance policy, already lower-volume withdrawals trigger a costly intervention. Therefore, a depositor believes a value-reducing intervention occurs more frequently,22and her relative incentive to withdraw to secure the deposit increases.
  In summary, whenever the RA shows less regulatory forbearance, the depositors trade off the calming effect whereby, given an intervention, the guaranteed pro rata shares under a mandatory stay are higher, against the alarming effect whereby a value-reducing intervention is believed to occur more frequently. Figure4depicts this trade-off.
  The U-shape of the trigger reveals that for conservative forbearance levels, the fear of intervention dominates the stop-loss effect such that the intervention makes the depositors' race for the exit more instead of less extreme. The stop-loss effect dominates only for lax regulatory forbearance, and intervention reduces the bank-run likelihood ex ante. The more lax the intervention regime, the weaker the fear of intervention. This finding holds because under conservative regulatory forbearance, the intervention causes more instantaneous asset liquidation and a larger reduction in deposit value, in comparison to a more lax intervention regime in which the RA seizes fewer assets, such that the intervention causes less additional liquidation. Therefore, the local change in the depositors' incentives at the intervention threshold is weaker under lax than under conservative intervention; that is, the downward jump of the payoff-difference function is less extreme. The fear of intervention vanishes completely under sufficiently lax intervention such that only the stop-loss effect remains. This finding holds because under lax policy, high-volume withdrawals and the correspondingly required asset liquidation push the payoff to roll over down to the insured value of the deposit even though the withdrawals have not reached the intervention threshold (see the lowest drawing in Figure2and the orange line in Figure3). As the withdrawals reach the intervention threshold, the intervention leaves the payoffs to roll over constant at the insured level; that is, inequality (10) holds with equality. Because the intervention no longer reduces the depositors' payoffs, the depositors are no longer afraid of an intervention, and a depositor's incentive to preempt the intervention vanishes.23
article.III. Resolution under Receivership:
In this section, I show that preemptive depositor behavior occurs not only under a PCA resolution, but also under a resolution under receivership where the regulator continues investment in assets after suspending the convertibility of deposits. For the sake of brevity, I henceforth call this resolution type a “receivership resolution.” In the context of the receivership resolution, I call the regulatory intervention to suspend the convertibility of deposits for taking the bank into receivership a “receivership intervention.”
  As in the case of PCA, under a receivership resolution the RA intervenes to resolve the bank if the aggregate withdrawal requests exceed the valueaL/Z1$aL/Z_1$. But given an intervention, the RA continues the investment of the share(1−a)$(1-a)$of assets until maturity int=2$t=2$, instead of liquidating these assets int=1$t=1$under PCA.24I impose that, unlike the bank, the RA is not an investment expert and therefore realizes a reduced returnr∈(0,H]$r \in (0,H]$on the remaining investment if the asset pays off.25Callrthe RA's exogenous investment-management skill.   As in the case of a PCA intervention, regulatory forbearance has two opposing effects on the depositors' preferences. First, if the RA intervenes more conservatively, she protects more assets from liquidation, so that at a fixed state, the pro rata shares under a mandatory stay are on average higher (stop-loss effect). Second, the intervention reduces resources to depositors because the asset's management switches from the bank's expert levelHto the RA's lower skill levelr(unconditional intervention externality). article.IV. Policy Goals: Efficiency versus Stab:
The previous sections have shown that the maximization of bank stability may require a certain degree of regulatory forbearance. In this section, I shift attention to an alternate policy goal, namely, the maximization of investment efficiency (value of bank investment), and discuss to what extent a regulator faces a trade-off between bank stability and efficiency maximization.
  
  Definition 2. (Efficient PCA policy)A PCA resolution policy isefficientif, given the policy announcement(a,γ)$(a,\gamma )$, the depositors coordinate on a run if and only if asset liquidation is efficient,θb(a,γ)=θe$\theta _b(a,\gamma )=\theta _e$.
  
  Generically, two types of inefficiencies can arise. First, if the critical state that follows a policy(a,γ)$(a,\gamma )$undercuts the efficiency cutoff,θb<θe$\theta _b<\theta _e$, state realizations in(θb,θe)$(\theta _b,\theta _e)$exist for which the depositors do not withdraw from the bank and investment continues even though the asset quality is low (“inefficient continuation of investment”). This inefficiently low propensity to run causes inefficiently high losses to the deposit-insurance fund for which the depositors ultimately have to pay via higher ex ante taxation (see also AppendixC). Because in this paper no alternative mechanism exists that might enforce asset liquidation when depositors do not withdraw, less bank stability, that is, a higher run propensity, can increase efficiency.
  As the second inefficiency type, if the policy(a,γ)$(a,\gamma )$implies a critical state that exceeds the efficient liquidation cutoff,θb>θe$\theta _b>\theta _e$, an interval of states(θe,θb)$(\theta _e,\theta _b)$exists at which the depositors run on the bank to trigger an intervention with a subsequent asset liquidation, even though the asset is of high quality (“inefficient run”). A lower run propensity would increase the value of investment because costly liquidation was prevented more often.
  The following corollary summarizes efficient forbearance levels, depending on the provision of deposit insurance.

  The provision of deposit insurance monotonically reduces the depositors' run propensity relative to the critical state (see LemmaB.4) and therefore determines efficient regulatory forbearance. The proof of Corollary1follows directly from LemmaB.4and Proposition2.
  The corollary says the stability and the efficiency maximizer coincide for low levels of insurance but differ for high insurance levels. For an illustration, the points in Figures7(a)and7(c)mark the forbearance levels at which, for a given level of deposit insurance, efficiency is maximized. Recall that at a given level of insurance, efficiency is maximized at the forbearance level that minimizes the distance between the efficiency cutoffθe$\theta _e$and the critical state curveθb(a)$\theta _b(a)$, that is, the depositors' run propensity as a function of regulatory forbearance. Figures7(c)and7(d)show the discrepancy between the efficiency- (black) and the bank stability- (red) maximizing forbearance level as a function of deposit insurance. To understand the emergence of the fork in these graphs, consider the corollary. It states that for intermediate levels of insurance, first-best efficiency (but not the stability maximum) is attained at two distinct forbearance levelsae1<ae2$a_{e1}<a_{e2}$simultaneously, and these forbearance levels also form stability mirrors. The fork occurs due to preemptive depositor behavior and illustrates that for a given level of deposit insurance, lax intervention atae2$a_{e2}$can attain the same level of stability and efficiency as conservative intervention atae1$a_{e1}$, while implying distinct tolerance levels for bank asset liquidation until regulatory intervention occurs. As insurance increases, the fork fans out, meaning that the spread in regulation intensity across the stability and efficiency mirrors,|ae2−ae1|$|a_{e2}-a_{e1}|$becomes more extreme. This effect arises because as insurance increases, the run propensity drops at every level of regulatory forbearance, so that for high-enough insurance levels, the U-shaped critical state curve is pushed down through the efficiency cutoff; see left panel.
  The proof of Corollary1is intuitive: for low levels of deposit-insurance coverage, depositors are sensitive to bad news on the asset's fundamental because they potentially face a full loss of their deposit when choosing the “wrong” action. They withdraw too often, such that inefficient runs may occur. That is, the critical state curve is entirely located above the efficiency cutoff (see LemmaB.4and Figures7(a)and7(c). To make inefficient runs less likely ex ante, the RA chooses the forbearance policy that minimizes the depositors' propensity to withdraw. Therefore, as long as deposit insurance is low, the maximization of bank stability and efficiency coincide. In particular, because the depositors exhibit preemptive behavior, that is, because the critical state (run propensity) as a function of regulatory forbearance has a U-shape, the run propensity is lowest and efficiency is highest at the interior forbearance levela∗$a^*$(see Proposition2). Consequently, no regulatory incentive exists to intervene more conservatively than at a forbearance levela∗$a^*$, because such an intervention would reduce both bank stability and efficiency.
  As deposit-insurance coverage increases, the depositors become more relaxed when observing information about the fundamental. They withdraw less often, implying a downward shift in the critical state curve (see LemmaB.4). For sufficiently high insurance coverage, the critical state curve therefore runs through the efficiency cutoff and intersects twice, where the double intersection is caused by preemptive depositor behavior, that is, the U-shape of the depositors' run propensityθb(a)$\theta _b(a)$in regulatory forbearance. Consequently, two distinct forbearance levels (stability mirrors),ae1≠ae2,ae1<ae2$ a_{e1} \ne a_{e2}, \, a_{e1} < a_{e2}$, exist that both attain the first-best level of efficiency. Conservative intervention atae1$a_{e1}$is just as efficient and stable as more lax intervention atae2$a_{e2}$. By the U-shape of the critical state curve, the global bank stability maximuma∗$a^*$is located between the two efficiency maximizers,ae1,ae2$a_{e1},a_{e2}$. Therefore, a regulator would face a trade-off between maximizing bank stability and efficiency.
  Under high insurance coverage, the critical state curve shifts further down and, given the miminum forbearance levela̲>0$\underline{a}>0$, is entirely located below the efficiency cutoff for every level of regulatory forbearance. Intuitively, for high insurance coverage, the depositors face minimal losses given a resolution and are therefore insensitive to bad news about the bank's solvency. They roll over their deposits even for severe solvency shocks on the bank, so that investment in the asset is continued inefficiently often,θb(a)<θe$\theta _b(a)<\theta _e$, for every forbearance level (see Figure7(a)). A higher run propensity was desirable to prevent both unnecessary losses to the deposit-insurance fund and a high ex ante taxation of the depositors for financing the fund. Because the critical state curve is U-shaped in forbearance, the distance to the efficiency cutoff is now minimized when the critical state is maximized (stability is minimized). A regulator who aims at maximizing bank stability would therefore simultaneously minimize efficiency, and vice versa, so that both goals are at odds with one another. Depending on the exogenous minimum forbearance levela̲∈(0,1)$\underline{a} \in (0,1)$, the efficient forbearance level can be located at either of the boundaries{a̲,1}$\lbrace \underline{a},1\rbrace$.
  If the RA can set both the forbearance level and the level of deposit-insurance coverage, she can always attain the first-best level of efficiency. The reason is that by LemmaB.5, the critical state curve can always be arranged to transition through the efficiency cutoff by carefully choosing the deposit-insurance level. The policy that implements the first-best level under a PCA intervention is not unique, as the forks in Figures7(b)and7(d)illustrate. In fact, infinitely many pairs(a,γ)$(a,\gamma )$exist that attain the first-best outcome. In particular, a laissez-faire policya=1$a=1$can attain the first-best outcome when fine-tuning the extent of deposit-insurance provision, which would make the existence of a regulatory intervention authority redundant. If, on the other hand, the RA sets both forbearance and deposit insurance to maximize bank stability, full deposit insurance reduces the run likelihood to zero for any forbearance levela∈(a̲,1]$a \in (\underline{a},1]$.
  The design of an efficient receivership resolution is more intricate than the design of an efficient PCA intervention. With both intervention types, regulatory forbearance impacts the depositors' run propensityθb$\theta _b$, and as a consequence, inefficient runs or inefficient continuation of investment caused by the absence of runs can arise. But one might believe the receivership intervention is superior to the PCA intervention in terms of efficiency because the former protects assets from liquidation and protects more assets the less regulatory forbearance is shown. Therefore, inefficient runs should be less harmful when a receivership-resolution mechanism is in place. This belief, however, neglects that assets are risky. The protection of assets increases efficiency only if the asset is of high quality. Less regulatory forbearance with a receivership intervention therefore improves efficiency if the true asset quality is high,θ>θe$\theta >\theta _e$, but lowers efficiency if the asset quality is low. Because the RA commits to her policy without observing the true asset quality,31the RA needs to balance the risk of protecting low-quality assets from liquidation with the risk of not protecting high-quality assets from liquidation. Balancing these two risks creates a complex trade-off and requires the RA to form an expectation of the average asset quality she protects given a run,E[θ|θ<θb(a)]$\mathbb {E}[\theta | \theta <\theta _b(a)]$, while internalizing the effect of her own regulatory forbearance on the run propensityθb(a)$\theta _b(a)$, and thus the chance that runs occur.
  One could say that two causes of inefficient continuation of investment exist in the case of the receivership resolution. The first cause is the absence of a run when the asset quality is low, and the second cause is the implementation of a conservative regulatory forbearance policy that protects many assets from liquidation even though the expected asset quality given an intervention is low. By contrast, regulatory forbearance does not cause this two-sided inefficiency in the case of the PCA intervention because the entire asset is liquidated in the course of the resolution irrespective of the forbearance level.32

article.V. Conclusion:
Regulatory interventions to suspend the convertibility of demand deposits have been widely discussed in the literature as a means to prevent bank runs. If the intervention subsequently protects the bank's investment from costly liquidation (“receivership resolution”), it guarantees a minimum continuation value to the invested deposit. However, this story is not complete. I show that receivership resolution comes with a considerable drawback if bank assets are risky and if the regulator is not as skilled as the bank in managing assets. In this case, receivership resolution can backfire by causing a probabilistic form of front-running by the depositors. Bank stability is maximized at an intermediate intervention delay. Neither laissez-faire nor an aggressive conservative intervention policy is optimal for minimizing the run propensity.
  These results hold equivalently for PCA, where the regulator seizes and liquidates all of the remaining assets given an intervention. Trade-offs exist between the goals of maximizing efficiency and bank stability. These trade-offs depend on the degree of deposit insurance. Under low levels of insurance, policy that is too conservative maximizes neither stability nor efficiency. For high levels of deposit insurance, laissez-faire is always among the candidates for the efficiency-maximizing intervention policy, under both a PCA and a receivership resolution.
  The analysis casts doubt on the prudence of overly aggressive intervention policies because lax policies can be just as or even more stable and efficient.

article.Appendix C: Deposit-Insurance Fund:
Last, I clarify the impact of regulatory forbearance on the expected loss incurred by the deposit-insurance fund and the according tax that is required for financing insurance ex ante. The insurance fund can incur losses for two reasons, either due to a run or, when a run remains absent, to a failure of the asset to pay. Consider the case of a PCA resolution. CallS<0$S<0$the expected loss of the fund per unit invested in the contract.
  By the preemptive depositor behavior, that is, the nonmonotoncity of the critical state, and the asset's riskiness, I conclude the following:
  Corollary C.1.Neither the insurance fund's exposure nor the tax for financing insurance necessarily declines as regulatory forbearance becomes more conservative.
  
  Moreover, following the efficiency discussion in SectionIV, the expected loss of the deposit-insurance fund does not monotonically decline as the run likelihood goes down because inefficient continuation of investment can arise or become more likely. If the chance of a run,θb$\theta _b$, declines, the loss given a run is incurred less often, but investment in the asset is continued more frequently and for lower asset qualities. Therefore, the expected loss due to asset failure becomes more severe.
